column3: to leave a place, thing, or person, usually for ever
column4: By the time the rebel troops arrived, the village had already been abandoned.
column5: Abandon your worries and enjoy the moment
column6: The project was abandoned due to lack of funding.

column3: to become less strong
column4: The storm has started to abate.
column5: The noise from the party next door finally abated around midnight.
column6: I hope the rain will abate before our cricket match this evening.

column3: different from what is typical or usual, especially in an unacceptable way
column4: Sitting in a bathtub and singing show tunes all day long might be considered aberrant behavior
column5: The politician's aberrant behavior caused a scandal and damaged his reputation
column6: The scientist noticed an aberrant result in his experiment and knew he had to investigate further.

column3: morally very bad
column4: Racism of any kind is abhorrent to me
column5: The use of child labor in factories is abhorrent.
column6: The vandalism of historical monuments is considered abhorrent by many who value cultural heritage

column3: the place where someone lives
column4: I bought you some nice candlesticks for your new abode.
column5: His mind became a sanctuary, a sacred abode where creativity and imagination flourished.
column6: After a long day at work, I returned to my cozy abode, eager to relax and unwind.

column3: very bad or unpleasant
column4: The prisoners are forced to live in abominable conditions.
column5: The President described the killings as an abominable crime
column6: The weather was abominable, cold with wind and rain

column3: something that you dislike and disapprove of
column4: Cruelty to animals is an abomination.
column5: Her cooking experiment turned into an abomination of flavors, leaving everyone at the table grimacing.
column6: The decrepit building was an abomination to the once-vibrant neighborhood.

column3: slightly rough, and often used for cleaning surfaces
column4: You'll need a strong abrasive for cleaning this sink.
column5: The abrasive texture of the sandpaper smoothed down the rough edges of the wooden surface.
column6: The abrasive cleaning agent stripped away the grime from the kitchen tiles, leaving them sparkling clean.

column3: rude and unfriendly (person);
column4: She has a rather abrasive manner.
column5: His abrasive mannerisms often put people off, making it challenging to form lasting relationships.
column6: He was known for his abrasive personality, always quick to criticize and slow to offer praise.

column3: true, right, or the same in all situations and not depending on anything else
column4: Her contribution was better than most, but in absolute terms (= without comparing it with anything else) it was not very good.
column5: In matters of justice, there is an absolute truth that transcends personal beliefs.
column6: She held an absolute conviction that honesty was always the best policy.

column3: setting free from guilt, sin, or penalty; forgiveness of an offense
column4: The priest would hear confessions and give absolution for sins
column5: After years of guilt, she sought absolution through confession, hoping to find peace.
column6: The forgiveness offered by her friend was like a soothing balm, granting her absolution from past mistakes.

column3: very interested in something and not paying attention to anything else
column4: Lost in the pages of her favorite book, she was completely absorbed in the captivating story.
column5: The artist was so absorbed in his painting that he lost track of time and forgot to eat.
column6: During recess, the child became completely absorbed in the game of tag, running and laughing with pure joy

column3: existing as an idea, feeling, or quality, not as a material object
column4: He is good at explaining quite complex, abstract ideas in a nice simple way.
column5: Her artwork was characterized by bold abstract shapes and vibrant colors, evoking emotions rather than depicting reality.
column6: The concept of time became abstract as they wandered through the timeless landscape, where past, present, and future melded into one.

column3: not known or understood by many people
column4: Abstruse things are difficult to understand because they are so deep and intellectually challenging.
column5: The professor's lecture on quantum mechanics was so abstruse that most students struggled to grasp even the basic concepts.
column6: The author's novel delved into abstruse philosophical questions, challenging readers to contemplate the nature of existence.

column3: stupid and unreasonable, or silly in a humorous way
column4: Do I look absurd in this hat?
column5: The idea that cats could fly was utterly absurd, yet it sparked a whimsical conversation among friends.
column6: It seemed absurd to him that she would travel halfway across the world just for a cup of coffee, but her dedication to the perfect brew was undeniable.

column3: great plenty
column4: The area has an abundance of lakes, rivers, and streams to fish in.
column5: The garden was a riot of color, bursting with an abundance of flowers in full bloom.
column6: In the small coastal town, the fishermen returned each day with their boats laden with an abundance of fresh seafood.

column3: more than enough
column4: There is abundant evidence that cars have a harmful effect on the environment.
column5: The forest was filled with abundant wildlife, from deer grazing peacefully to birds singing in the treetops.
column6: Even in the midst of drought, the oasis provided an abundant source of water for the parched travelers.

column3: easy to understand or enjoy
column4: The instruction manual is accessible and does not require specialist knowledge.
column5: The museum's interactive exhibits made art history accessible and engaging for visitors of all ages.
column6: The author's clear writing style made the complex topic accessible to readers of all backgrounds.

column3: to praise enthusiastically and publicly
column4: She is being acclaimed as the greatest dancer of her generation.
column5: The actor received widespread acclaim for his powerful performance in the lead role.
column6: The newly released album quickly garnered critical acclaim, earning praise from music critics and fans alike.

column3: praise and approval
column4: She has sold millions of records and earned numerous awards and accolades over a 30-year career.
column5: Receiving the Nobel Prize was the ultimate accolade for her groundbreaking research.
column6: The prestigious award ceremony was attended by luminaries from various fields, each eager to receive their accolades.

column3: suggesting that you think someone has done something bad
column4: She gave me an accusatory look.
column5: Her tone grew increasingly accusatory as she questioned him about his whereabouts last night.
column6: The politician's speech was filled with accusatory remarks, blaming his opponents for the current state of affairs.

column3: to say that someone has done something morally wrong, illegal, or unkind
column4: He's been accused of robbery/murder.
column5: The prosecutor planned to accuse the suspect of embezzlement during the trial.
column6: She didn't hesitate to accuse her colleague of stealing her ideas during the brainstorming session.

column3: To make familiar by use; to familiarize
column4: It takes a while to accustom yourself to getting up early in the morning after a long summer school holiday.
column5: It took some time for the new puppy to accustom itself to its surroundings.
column6: Living in a foreign country, she had to accustom herself to different cultural norms and practices.

column3: Sharp, bitter, or harsh in temper, language.
column4: After John heard his teacher's acerbic comments, he was not motivated to complete his project.
column5: His acerbic wit often left others feeling stung by his sharp remarks.
column6: The critic's acerbic review tore apart the film, leaving its creators disheartened.

column3: knowing or being familiar with a person or something
column4: Police said the thieves were obviously well acquainted with the alarm system at the department store.
column5: She became acquainted with her new neighbors by attending the neighborhood welcome party.
column6: Before starting the project, it's essential to get acquainted with the relevant literature in the field.

column3: the act of accepting or agreeing to something, often unwillingly
column4: I was surprised by his acquiescence to play the piano in front of everyone.
column5: Her silence was interpreted as acquiescence, even though she harbored reservations about the proposed plan.
column6: His acquiescence to her demands surprised everyone, as he was known for his stubbornness.

column3: to get or obtain something
column4: From humble beginnings he went on to acquire great wealth.
column5: He hoped to acquire a new skill by enrolling in an online course.
column6: She decided to acquire a vintage car to fulfill her lifelong dream of restoring classic automobiles.

column3: full of anger, arguments, and bad feeling
column4: Their friendship ended eight years ago in an acrimonious argument.
column5: The negotiations between the two countries became acrimonious, with neither side willing to compromise on their demands
column6: The debate between the two political rivals grew acrimonious as they traded insults and accusations on live television.

column3: an abbreviation formed from the initial letters of other words and pronounced as a word
column4: NASA, an acronym for the National Aeronautics and Space Administration, spearheads space exploration efforts.
column5: The term 'UNICEF' is an acronym derived from the United Nations International Children's Emergency Fund.
column6: The military uses acronyms extensively to streamline communication and convey information efficiently, for example RADAR, which stands for Radio Detection and Ranging.

column3: very serious or severe; (of senses or feelings) very sharp and strong
column4: She felt acute embarrassment at his behaviour.
column5: The doctor diagnosed the patient with acute appendicitis, requiring immediate surgery.
column6: The team faced an acute shortage of resources, threatening the success of the project.

column3: to give attention to or deal with a matter or problem
column4: We'll address that question at the next meeting.
column5: The mayor will address the concerns of the community during the town hall meeting.
column6: It's important to address the issue of climate change through sustainable practices.

column3: in a way that is enough or satisfactory for a particular purpose
column4: While some patients can be adequately cared for at home, others are best served by care in a hospital.
column5: She felt adequately prepared for the exam after studying diligently for weeks.
column6: The company provided its employees with adequately sized cubicles to ensure comfortable working spaces.

column3: to stick firmly to a surface
column4: Glue won't adhere to any surface that's wet.
column5: It's crucial to adhere to safety protocols when working in hazardous environments.
column6: The chef instructed his apprentices to adhere strictly to the recipe's measurements for the perfect dish.

column3: Lying near, close, neighboring, bordering on.
column4: A field adjacent to the highway
column5: The hotel rooms were adjacent, allowing easy access for families traveling together.
column6: The two buildings stood adjacent to each other, creating a striking architectural contrast.

column3: to have a pause or rest during a formal meeting or trial
column4: Shall we adjourn for lunch?
column5: The judge decided to adjourn the trial until the following week due to unforeseen circumstances.
column6: After hours of intense negotiations, the meeting was finally adjourned, with no resolution reached.

column3: deserving respect or approval
column4: The police did an admirable job in keeping the fans calm.
column5: Her dedication to charity work is truly admirable, inspiring others to give back to their communities.
column6: The athlete's perseverance in the face of adversity is admirable, earning her the respect of her peers.

column3: the act of agreeing that something is true, especially unwillingly
column4: By his own admission, he is not studying much.
column5: His admission of guilt shocked everyone in the courtroom.
column6: The admission of wrongdoing was the first step toward reconciliation in their relationship.

column3: to accept or start to use something new
column4: I think it's time to adopt a different strategy to conquer the next level in this game.
column5: The company plans to adopt new technology to improve efficiency in its production process.
column6: The government aims to adopt a new policy to address environmental concerns and promote sustainability.

column3: to love someone very much, especially in a way that shows a lot of admiration or respect, or to like something very much
column4: She has one son and she adores him.
column5: She adores her grandmother and cherishes every moment spent with her.
column6: The singer's fans absolutely adore her, showing unwavering support at every concert.

column3: very great admiration or praise for someone, especially when it is more than is deserved
column4: As a born performer, she loves the excitement and she loves the adulation.
column5: The actor was overwhelmed by the adulation of his fans at the movie premiere.
column6: Despite the adulation from the public, the artist remained humble and grounded.

column3: to go or move something forward, or to develop or improve something
column4: We have advanced greatly in our knowledge of the universe.
column5: The scientist made significant advances in cancer research, bringing hope to millions of patients.
column6: The army began to advance towards the enemy's stronghold, prepared for a fierce battle.

column3: someone or something that opposes or competes with another
column4: He saw her as his main adversary within the company.
column5: In chess, your opponent is not your enemy but your adversary
column6: The superhero faced a formidable adversary in the form of a villain with extraordinary powers.

column3: having a negative or harmful effect on something
column4: It is clear that these violent films have an adverse effect on children.
column5: The adverse weather conditions forced the cancellation of the outdoor event.
column6: The company faced adverse effects on its reputation following the scandal.

column3: relating to the enjoyment or study of beauty
column4: furniture that is both aesthetic and functional
column5: The minimalist aesthetic of the room exuded a sense of calm and tranquility
column6: She appreciated the artistic aesthetic of the city's architecture, with its blend of old and modern styles.

column3: friendly and easy to talk to
column4: He struck me as an affable sort of a man.
column5: The host greeted each guest with an affable smile, making everyone feel welcome.
column6: Despite his busy schedule, he always found time to engage in affable conversation with coworkers.

column3: showing feelings of liking or love
column4: He's an affectionate little boy.
column5: The elderly couple shared an affectionate embrace, demonstrating their enduring love for each other.
column6: Their affectionate gestures, such as holding hands and gentle touches, spoke volumes about their relationship.

column3: a liking or sympathy for someone or something, especially because of shared characteristics
column4: Some cat breeds have a natural affinity for water, unlike most cat breeds.
column5: She felt an immediate affinity for the quaint seaside town, drawn to its charm and tranquility.
column6: His affinity for mathematics led him to pursue a career in engineering.

column3: the act of saying yes or of showing that you mean yes
column4: She was nodding in affirmation.
column5: Receiving positive feedback from her peers was a powerful affirmation of her skills and abilities.
column6: The teacher provided words of affirmation to her students, fostering a supportive and encouraging classroom environment.

column3: something that makes you suffer
column4: Malnutrition is one of the common afflictions of the poor.
column5: His chronic back pain was a constant affliction, making it difficult for him to perform everyday tasks.
column6: The region was struck by a severe drought, causing affliction to farmers and communities reliant on agriculture.

column3: having a lot of money or owning a lot of things
column4: The affluent neighborhood boasted sprawling mansions and manicured lawns.
column5: As an affluent businessman, he enjoyed a luxurious lifestyle filled with exotic vacations and fine dining.
column6: The affluent donor's generous contribution helped fund the construction of the new community center.
